questions.question: Explain the difference between BFS and DFS. Which data structures do they use and why?
keyPoints: BFS uses Queue (FIFO), explores level by level
DFS uses Stack (LIFO) or recursion, explores depth-first
BFS finds shortest path in unweighted graphs
DFS is better for deep exploration, cycle detection, topological sort

questions.question: What is a connected component? How can you find all connected components in an undirected graph?
keyPoints: Connected component is a maximal set of vertices where each pair is connected by a path
Algorithm: Perform DFS/BFS from any unvisited vertex, mark all reachable vertices as one component
Repeat until all vertices are visited; count components

questions.question: Describe how BFS can be used to check if a graph is bipartite.
keyPoints: Bipartite graph can be colored with 2 colors such that adjacent vertices have different colors
Algorithm: BFS from any vertex, assign it color 1, alternate colors for neighbors
If any neighbor already has same color as current vertex, graph is not bipartite

questions.question: How can DFS be used to detect a cycle in an undirected graph?
keyPoints: During DFS, when exploring from vertex u to vertex v:
If v is already visited and v is not the parent of u, then a cycle exists
This is because an already visited non-parent vertex indicates a back edge forming a cycle

questions.question: What is topological sorting? Under what conditions is it possible?
keyPoints: Topological sort is linear ordering of vertices in DAG such that for every edge (u,v), u comes before v
Only possible for Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
Can be computed using DFS (push to stack when finished) or Kahn's algorithm (BFS-based)

questions.question: Compare adjacency matrix and adjacency list representations of graphs. When is each preferred?
keyPoints: Adjacency Matrix: O(V²) space, O(1) edge lookup, good for dense graphs
Adjacency List: O(V+E) space, O(degree) edge lookup, good for sparse graphs
Use adjacency list when E << V², use matrix when graph is dense

questions.question: Explain the concept of back edges in DFS and their significance.
keyPoints: Back edge is a directed edge (u,v) where v is an ancestor of u in DFS tree
Detection of a back edge indicates presence of a cycle in directed graph
Essential for algorithms like cycle detection and finding strongly connected components

questions.question: Write the BFS algorithm and explain how it finds the shortest path in an unweighted graph. Also discuss its time and space complexity.
keyPoints: Initialize queue with source vertex, mark as visited
While queue not empty: dequeue vertex, explore all unvisited neighbors, mark them visited and enqueue
For shortest path: first time vertex is discovered is via shortest path; maintain parent array to reconstruct path
Time: O(V+E), Space: O(V) for visited array and queue

questions.question: Write the DFS algorithm (both iterative and recursive). Explain how it can be used for: (a) Cycle detection (b) Topological sorting
keyPoints: Recursive: call DFS on unvisited neighbors, mark visited, add to result after exploring neighbors
Iterative: use explicit stack to simulate recursion
(a) Cycle: detect back edge during DFS - if visited vertex in current recursion stack, cycle exists
(b) Topological: push vertex to stack after all descendants explored, pop to get reverse topological order

questions.question: What are strongly connected components? Explain Kosaraju's or Tarjan's algorithm for finding them.
keyPoints: SCC is a maximal set of vertices where each vertex can reach every other vertex in the set
Kosaraju's Algorithm: (1) DFS on original graph, store finish times (2) Reverse graph (3) DFS on reversed graph in decreasing finish times, each tree is an SCC
Time: O(V+E), Space: O(V)
Alternative: Tarjan's algorithm using low-link values

questions.question: Discuss the applications of BFS and DFS. Which algorithm would you use for: (i) Finding if path exists (ii) Finding shortest path (iii) Cycle detection (iv) Topological sort
keyPoints: BFS Applications: shortest path in unweighted graphs, level-order traversal, finding connected components, bipartite checking
DFS Applications: cycle detection, topological sort, path finding, finding strongly connected components
(i) Either - DFS or BFS works (ii) BFS (iii) DFS (iv) DFS (or Kahn's algorithm with queue)
